content.text: Choosing between integration and end-to-end tests in an agentic AI system is not a style preference — it is an architectural decision that determines how quickly failures are detected, how precisely they are localized, and how much each CI run costs. The two test types have fundamentally different scopes, assertions, and economic profiles, and a well-designed test suite uses both in the proportions determined by the test pyramid.
What it is

Integration tests in agentic AI systems exercise the collaboration boundary between two or three adjacent components: a prompt builder and an LLM API call, an LLM response and a tool executor, or a tool result and a parser. The scope is intentionally narrow. The LLM API is either stubbed with a recorded cassette or called with a minimal, low-cost model. Assertions verify structural correctness: does the model return a valid JSON tool call, does the executor receive the correct arguments, does the parser produce a typed output without throwing.

End-to-end (E2E) scenario tests exercise a complete agent workflow from initial user input to final response, with a real orchestration loop, real model calls, and real (or staging) external tools. The SUT is the entire application stack. Assertions verify goal-level correctness: did the agent complete the assigned task, stay within turn-count bounds, avoid tool errors, and produce output in the expected format? E2E tests deliberately ignore the internal mechanics; they treat the agent as a black box.
Why it matters

Integration tests and E2E tests fail for different reasons and reveal different classes of bugs. Integration tests catch structural API contract violations — a tool schema field renamed in a code refactor, a model response format change after a provider upgrade, a missing error handler in the retry logic. These bugs manifest in the first one or two component interactions; they do not require a multi-step scenario to surface.

E2E tests catch emergent failures that only appear when multiple components interact over several turns: an agent that correctly calls each individual tool but selects them in an order that produces a circular dependency, a planning prompt that works for three-step tasks but loses context on step seven, or a system message update that subtly changes tool-selection probabilities. No integration test, however comprehensive, can catch these because they arise from the composition of components, not from individual components in isolation.

Over-investing in E2E tests at the expense of integration tests is a common mistake. An E2E failure with no corresponding integration failure tells the developer that something went wrong somewhere across ten API calls and four tool executions — the debugging search space is enormous. An integration test failure pinpoints the exact component and input that caused the regression within seconds.
Benefits
Integration test benefits
Deterministic: cassette replay makes the LLM response identical on every run.
Fast: typical runtime is 0.5–5 seconds per test, enabling hundreds of tests per CI run.
Cheap: cassette-replayed tests incur zero token cost after the initial recording.
Precise: a failure names the exact component and call that broke.
Safe to parallelize: no shared mutable state, no concurrency conflicts.
E2E test benefits
Validates the full agent behavior that the user actually experiences.
Catches emergent orchestration failures invisible to integration tests.
Exercises the real model with the real system prompt — no abstraction leakage.
Provides a behavioral regression baseline after model or prompt upgrades.
Doubles as living documentation of supported user scenarios.
Trade-offs
Integration tests are blind to multi-step emergent failures; a perfect integration suite gives false confidence if the orchestration logic is broken.
E2E tests are slow (10–120 seconds each), expensive (real token spend), and inherently flaky because LLM output is non-deterministic.
Integration tests require cassette maintenance: when the LLM API response format changes, cassettes must be re-recorded.
E2E tests require scenario design expertise: a poorly designed scenario either over-specifies (brittle) or under-specifies (misses real failures).
Both test types grow in maintenance cost as the agent's tool surface and system prompt evolve.
How it works
Integration test mechanics

An integration test constructs the minimal input needed to exercise one collaboration boundary, fires the real (or replayed) API call, and asserts on the structured response. The key technique is cassette-based HTTP replay: the first run calls the live LLM API and records the request and response to a YAML fixture file. Subsequent runs replay the recorded response without network I/O. This makes the test deterministic, free, and offline-capable.
For tool execution integration tests, the LLM response is hardcoded or replayed, and the test exercises only the tool executor with that response. This isolates executor bugs from LLM behavior.
E2E test mechanics

An E2E scenario test instantiates the full agent with a real (or staging) model, sends a carefully designed user message, and waits for the agent to reach a terminal state (completed, error, or max_turns exceeded). The assertion is made against the final state object, not against any intermediate step. Timeouts are mandatory to prevent infinite agent loops from blocking CI.

Scenario tests should be parameterized across a small set of representative inputs that cover distinct agent code paths: the happy-path case, the clarification-needed case, the tool-error-recovery case, and the context-limit case. Avoiding overlapping scenarios keeps the suite small and each test meaningful.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Side-by-side architecture diagram comparing integration test scope (single LLM call + tool) versus E2E test scope (full agent loop)

content.text: The diagram contrasts the bounded scope of an integration test — limited to the prompt builder, one LLM call, and one tool execution — against the unbounded scope of an E2E scenario test that spans the full orchestration loop and produces a final response. The key architectural insight is that the integration test can be determinized through cassette replay, while the E2E test is inherently tied to live model behavior.
When to use it
Prefer integration tests when
You have changed a prompt template and need to verify the model still returns a valid tool call.
You have updated a tool schema and need to verify the model correctly populates the new required fields.
You have modified the error handling in the API client and need to verify retry logic.
You are running on a CI budget that cannot afford per-PR token spend.
Prefer E2E tests when
You have changed the system prompt and need to verify end-to-end behavioral correctness.
You have upgraded to a new model version and need a behavioral regression check.
You have added a new agent capability (new tool, new planning step) and need to verify the orchestration loop handles it.
You are running acceptance tests before a production release.
How to implement

The implementation strategy for both test types relies on a shared fixture layer that abstracts the agent configuration and environment. Integration tests plug into this fixture with cassette replay enabled; E2E tests plug in with real API credentials and a staging model.
Examples

# tests/e2e/scenarios/test_research_workflow.py
import pytest
from myagent.agent import ResearchAgent

RESEARCH_SCENARIOS = [
    (
        "factual_lookup",
        "What is the current atmospheric CO2 concentration in parts per million?",
        ["co2", "ppm", "concentration"],
        5,
    ),
    (
        "multi_step_synthesis",
        "Compare renewable energy adoption rates in Germany and France since 2015.",
        ["germany", "france", "renewable"],
        10,
    ),
]

@pytest.mark.e2e
@pytest.mark.timeout(120)
@pytest.mark.parametrize("name,query,expected_keywords,max_turns", RESEARCH_SCENARIOS)
def test_research_scenarios(staging_config, name, query, expected_keywords, max_turns):
    agent = ResearchAgent(config=staging_config)
    result = agent.run(query)

    assert result.status == "completed", (
        f"Scenario '{name}' did not complete. Status: {result.status}, "
        f"error: {getattr(result, 'error', None)}"
    )
    assert result.turn_count <= max_turns, (
        f"Scenario '{name}' exceeded {max_turns} turns (used {result.turn_count})"
    )
    answer_lower = result.answer.lower()
    missing = [kw for kw in expected_keywords if kw not in answer_lower]
    assert not missing, (
        f"Scenario '{name}' answer missing expected keywords: {missing}"
    )
Real-world example

A customer support automation platform operates a multi-tool agent that queries a ticket database, retrieves knowledge-base articles, generates draft replies, and escalates edge cases to human agents. The integration test suite covers 95 test cases across four tool boundaries: ticket API client, KB search API, draft generator, and escalation classifier. Each cassette is re-recorded weekly against the staging LLM to catch silent API format changes. The suite runs in 48 seconds on every pull request.

The E2E scenario suite covers 14 named support scenarios: billing dispute, password reset, product defect report, and so on. Each runs against a staging GPT-4o endpoint. A timeout of 90 seconds is enforced per scenario. The suite runs nightly and on releases, not on every PR, to avoid excessive token spend. The separation keeps PR feedback under five minutes while ensuring weekly scenario-level regression coverage.

When the LLM provider changed the tool-call response format from function_call to tool_calls in a breaking API upgrade, the integration tests caught the breakage within 24 hours of re-recording cassettes against the new API version. The E2E tests also caught it, but only during the next nightly run — and the integration tests' structured assertions pinpointed the parser as the broken component, rather than requiring manual trace analysis of a full agent run.
Common mistakes
Asserting on LLM response content in integration tests: LLM output is non-deterministic even with replay; assert on structure, not semantics.
Running E2E tests on every commit: slow feedback loop and runaway token costs accumulate quickly at team scale.
Forgetting to scrub PII from cassette fixtures before committing: recorded API responses may contain user data.
Designing E2E scenarios that overlap significantly: ten similar factual-lookup scenarios do not provide more coverage than two well-designed ones.
Setting E2E scenario timeouts too low: agents that need to reason across multiple turns will hit artificial limits and appear to fail non-deterministically.
Not tagging test types with pytest markers: mixing integration and E2E in the same run defeats the purpose of tiered CI gating.
Best practices
Record cassettes against a real LLM call, then commit them to the repository as test fixtures — they serve as precise API contract documentation.
Re-record cassettes on a schedule (weekly or before each release) to catch silent LLM API changes early.
Use pytest markers (@pytest.mark.integration, @pytest.mark.e2e) to gate each layer in CI.
Keep E2E scenarios to 10–25 total; each should represent a distinct user journey, not a variation of the same workflow.
Make E2E assertions behavioral, not textual: assert on result structure and keyword presence, not exact phrasing.
Run integration tests in strict isolation mode (no network, cassette replay only) and E2E tests with STAGING_API_KEY to make the distinction explicit.
Performance & scalability

Integration tests scale horizontally without limit: they share no state and make no live network calls when cassette replay is active. A suite of 200 integration tests can complete in under 30 seconds with 8-way parallelism. The bottleneck is disk I/O reading cassette files, not CPU or network.

E2E tests are limited by model throughput. Running 20 concurrent E2E tests against a single API key will hit rate limits. A practical approach is to run E2E tests serially or with a concurrency of 3–5, and to provision a dedicated CI API key with a higher rate-limit tier. At 30 seconds per test and a concurrency of 4, a 20-test suite completes in about 2.5 minutes — acceptable for a nightly or release-gate job.
Tooling & ecosystem
vcrpy
: Python library for recording and replaying HTTP interactions; the standard for integration test cassettes.
respx
: async-native HTTP mocking library for httpx clients; ideal for modern async LLM clients.
pytest-recording
: pytest plugin wrapping vcrpy; provides @pytest.mark.vcr() decorator for cassette-per-test management.
pytest-asyncio
: enables async test functions; mandatory when the LLM client uses async I/O.
pytest-timeout
: enforces per-test time limits; critical for E2E tests with potentially looping agents.
nock (Node.js)
: HTTP intercept library for JavaScript/TypeScript integration tests; equivalent to vcrpy.
msw (Mock Service Worker)
: browser and Node HTTP mocking; useful for full-stack E2E tests that include the frontend.

Security & reliability

Integration test cassette files must be treated as sensitive artifacts. A cassette records the exact HTTP request including authorization headers. Before committing cassettes, scrub the Authorization header and any Bearer tokens from the YAML fixture using a pre-commit hook or a cassette post-processor. Many teams replace real API keys in cassettes with a placeholder string during recording.

E2E tests that run against a staging LLM must use a dedicated staging API key with usage limits set to prevent runaway spend. The key should be rotated on a monthly schedule and stored as an encrypted CI secret, never hardcoded. E2E tests should include an adversarial scenario that sends a prompt injection attempt to verify the agent's safety guardrails hold under real model conditions.
Key takeaways
Integration tests and E2E tests are complementary, not substitutes: each catches a distinct class of failures.
Integration tests verify structural API correctness at the component boundary using cassette replay for determinism and zero cost.
E2E scenario tests verify goal-level behavioral correctness using a real model and real orchestration loop.
Over-investing in E2E tests at the expense of integration tests increases CI cost and reduces failure localization.
Cassettes should be treated as API contract fixtures, re-recorded on schedule to catch upstream format changes.
Use pytest markers and CI stage gates to run each test type at the appropriate frequency: integration on every PR, E2E on merges and releases.
E2E scenario count should stay small (10-25); each scenario must represent a genuinely distinct user journey to justify its cost.
